questions.question:
The ninth and the tenth of this month are Monday and Tuesday ________.

A. figuratively  B. retrospectively  C. respectively  D. rightfully
questions.to_find: the adverb that correctly completes the sentence pairing two items in order

understand.plain: The sentence lists two items (ninth and tenth) alongside two other items (Monday and Tuesday) in the same sequence. The missing word must mean 'in the order just mentioned'. The word 'respectively' is specifically used to link two parallel lists item-by-item in sequence. The other options either convey manner, time reference, or moral justness — none of which fits here.

keywords.explain: An adverb meaning 'in the order already mentioned'; used to match two parallel lists element by element.
keywords.example: Ram and Shyam scored 90 and 85 respectively — i.e., Ram scored 90, Shyam scored 85.

keywords.explain: Means 'in a non-literal or metaphorical sense'; has nothing to do with pairing sequential items.
Means 'looking back at past events'; relates to time, not item-order correspondence.
keywords.example: The law was applied retrospectively to earlier cases.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely a vocabulary/grammar question.

given.aim: Choose the adverb that correctly maps two dates to two weekdays in sequence

terms.meaning: Two calendar dates listed in order
terms.example: The ninth of June and the tenth of June

terms.meaning: Two weekday names listed in the same order
terms.example: Monday = first weekday, Tuesday = second weekday
terms.connects: Second parallel list (weekdays), paired with dates

terms.meaning: The word that links both lists item-by-item: ninth = Monday, tenth = Tuesday
terms.example: Respectively performs this exact function in English

given.plan: Identify which adverb signals a one-to-one correspondence between two parallel lists; 'respectively' is the standard English word for this purpose.

steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.apply: The sentence has two parallel lists: (ninth, tenth) and (Monday, Tuesday). The blank must connect them in order: ninth -> Monday, tenth -> Tuesday.
steps.note: This is a classic 'respectively' usage pattern in English.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Figuratively = metaphorical sense (wrong). Retrospectively = looking back in time (wrong). Rightfully = with moral justness (wrong). Only 'respectively' means 'in the order mentioned'.

solution.result: C. respectively — the ninth is Monday and the tenth is Tuesday, in that order.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a vocabulary and contextual usage question.

questions.question:
As the two speakers become increasingly agitated, the debate became _________.

A. lukewarm  B. poetic  C. forgiving  D. heated
questions.to_find: the adjective that best describes a debate whose participants are becoming increasingly agitated

understand.plain: The sentence says the speakers are becoming more and more agitated (worked up, angry). The debate's tone must reflect this escalating emotion. 'Heated' is used idiomatically in English to describe an argument or debate that is intense and emotionally charged. Lukewarm means unenthusiastic, poetic means lyrical, and forgiving means lenient — none of these align with agitation.

keywords.explain: Feeling or appearing troubled and anxious; emotionally stirred up or angry.
keywords.example: The customer grew agitated when her complaint was ignored.

keywords.explain: A common English collocation meaning a debate that is intense, passionate, and emotionally charged.
keywords.example: A heated argument broke out between the two politicians over the budget.

keywords.explain: Mildly warm; figuratively means showing little enthusiasm or interest — the opposite of agitated.
keywords.example: The audience gave a lukewarm response to the proposal.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary in context question.

given.aim: Find the adjective that correctly characterizes a debate where speakers are increasingly agitated

terms.meaning: Progressively more emotionally worked up and angry
terms.example: Tempers rising as the exchange continues
terms.connects: Cause — sets the emotional tone of the debate

terms.meaning: A formal or informal discussion where opposing views are exchanged
terms.example: A televised debate between two candidates
terms.connects: Subject being described by the blank adjective

terms.meaning: Must describe the intensifying emotional temperature of the debate
terms.example: Heated is the standard English collocation for an intense, emotional debate
terms.connects: Effect — how the debate is characterized as a result of the agitation

given.plan: Match the cause (agitation) to its natural linguistic effect; in English, 'heated' directly collocates with 'debate/argument' to mean emotionally intense.

steps.title: Analyze the causal relationship
steps.apply: Increasing agitation => the debate gets more intense and emotionally charged, not calmer (eliminating lukewarm, poetic, forgiving).
steps.note: Lukewarm and forgiving imply a cooling effect, opposite of agitation.

steps.title: Apply collocation knowledge
steps.apply: In English, 'a heated debate/argument' is a fixed collocation meaning an intense, emotion-driven exchange. Agitated speakers produce a heated debate.
steps.note: The word 'heated' metaphorically mirrors the rising temperature of emotions.

solution.result: D. heated — an increasingly agitated debate is idiomatically called 'heated' in English.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a vocabulary and collocation question.

questions.question:
If you choose plan P, you will have to _________ plan Q, as these two are mutually _________.

A. forgo, exclusive  B. forget, inclusive  C. accept, exhaustive  D. adopt, intrusive
questions.to_find: the pair of words that correctly completes the sentence about mutually exclusive plans

understand.plain: The sentence states that if you pick plan P, you must give up plan Q — implying you cannot have both. This is the definition of mutual exclusivity: two options that cannot coexist. 'Forgo' means to go without or give up, and 'exclusive' in the phrase 'mutually exclusive' means they cannot both be chosen simultaneously. The other pairs either contradict the logic or use the wrong words.

keywords.explain: To refrain from, abstain from, or give up something voluntarily.
keywords.example: She decided to forgo dessert to stick to her diet.

keywords.explain: Two events or options are mutually exclusive if they cannot both occur or be selected at the same time.
keywords.example: Getting heads and tails on the same coin flip are mutually exclusive outcomes.

keywords.explain: Including everything; or in math, an interval that includes its endpoints. The opposite of exclusive in this context.
keywords.example: The sale applies to all products, inclusive of accessories.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a double-blank vocabulary question.

given.aim: Identify the verb-adjective pair that logically completes a statement about two mutually exclusive plans

terms.meaning: Selecting one of the two mutually exclusive options
terms.example: Opting for Option A in a binary choice
terms.connects: The condition that forces abandonment of the other plan

terms.meaning: The action you must take regarding plan Q when you choose P — must mean 'give up'
terms.example: Forgo = to go without; abandon the other option

terms.meaning: The nature of the relationship between P and Q — must mean 'cannot coexist'
terms.example: Mutually exclusive = you can have one or the other, not both
terms.connects: Explains why choosing P forces you to forgo Q

given.plan: The logic is: Choosing P => must give up Q => because P and Q are mutually exclusive. Match 'give up' to 'forgo' and 'cannot coexist' to 'exclusive'.

steps.title: Fill blank 1 — find the correct verb
steps.apply: The verb must mean 'to give up or abandon'. Forgo = give up (correct). Forget = fail to remember (wrong context). Accept and adopt mean taking on, not giving up — contradicts the logic.
steps.note: Only 'forgo' conveys voluntary abstention from plan Q.

steps.title: Fill blank 2 — find the correct adjective
steps.apply: The adjective describes why choosing P forces you to give up Q. 'Mutually exclusive' is the standard phrase meaning 'cannot both be selected'. Inclusive = includes all (opposite). Exhaustive = covering all cases. Intrusive = invading others' space. Only 'exclusive' fits.
steps.note: 'Mutually exclusive' is a well-established term in logic, probability, and everyday English.

solution.result: A. forgo, exclusive — choosing P requires you to forgo Q, as they are mutually exclusive.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; 'mutually exclusive' appears in probability but here is tested as vocabulary.

questions.question:
"The increasing interest in tribal characters might be a mere coincidence, but the timing is of interest. None of this, though, is to say that the tribal hero has arrived in Hindi cinema, or that the new crop of characters represents the acceptance of the tribal character in the industry. The films and the characters are interesting; to say that they depict the tribal character accurately. The timing and the interest are intriguing, but the pattern is not yet representative enough to be described as a pattern."

What does the word 'arrived' mean in the paragraph above?

A. reached a terminus  B. came to a conclusion  C. came to a place  D. became successful
questions.to_find: the meaning of the word 'arrived' as used in the context of the given passage

understand.plain: In the passage, 'the tribal hero has arrived in Hindi cinema' means the tribal hero has come into (or established a presence in) Hindi cinema as a location or domain. The phrase 'arrived in Hindi cinema' is analogous to 'arrived in a city' — meaning reached or entered that space. The passage explicitly says this arrival is NOT yet confirmed, suggesting the tribal hero has not yet truly come to Hindi cinema as a mainstream presence.

keywords.explain: In the phrase 'arrived in Hindi cinema', 'arrived' means 'came to a place' — entered or established presence in that domain/space.
keywords.example: The artist arrived in Bollywood after years in regional cinema.

keywords.explain: Means reached the end point of a journey (like a train terminus); not the intended meaning here.
keywords.example: The train arrived at its terminus in Mumbai.

keywords.explain: Another idiomatic use of 'arrived' (to have arrived = to have achieved recognition), but here the preposition 'in Hindi cinema' points to a location meaning, not achievement.
keywords.example: After her debut film, she had truly arrived (= achieved fame).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading comprehension and vocabulary in context question.

given.aim: Determine the specific sense in which 'arrived' is used in the phrase 'the tribal hero has arrived in Hindi cinema'

terms.meaning: A protagonist character from a tribal background in a film
terms.example: A film featuring an Adivasi hero as the central character
terms.connects: The subject that has or has not 'arrived'

terms.example: Films made in the Hindi language for mass audiences
terms.connects: The 'place' or domain that the tribal hero has or has not entered

terms.meaning: Signals location — 'arrived in' a place, not 'arrived at' a conclusion
terms.example: He arrived in London vs. he arrived at a decision
terms.connects: Grammatical clue that 'arrived' here means movement to a location/domain

given.plan: Use the grammatical structure ('arrived in' + location) and the passage's meaning to identify that 'arrived' here means 'came to a place' — the tribal hero has not yet entered/established itself in Hindi cinema.

steps.title: Analyze the grammatical structure
steps.apply: 'Arrived in Hindi cinema' uses the preposition 'in' with a domain/place (Hindi cinema). This parallels 'arrived in London' or 'arrived in town', pointing to option C: 'came to a place'.
steps.note: When 'arrived' is followed by 'in + location', it means movement to that location.

steps.title: Check against context
steps.apply: The passage says it would be premature to say the tribal hero has 'arrived in Hindi cinema'. This means the tribal hero has not yet truly entered/established a presence in Hindi cinema as a mainstream domain.
steps.note: Option D (became successful) would use 'arrived' without a location phrase; the 'in' preposition disambiguates.

solution.result: C. came to a place — 'arrived in Hindi cinema' means the tribal hero has come to (established a presence in) Hindi cinema as a domain.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a reading comprehension and contextual word meaning question.

questions.question:
References : _________ :: Guidelines : Implement

(By word meaning)

A. Sight  B. Site  C. Cite  D. Plagiarise
questions.to_find: the word that pairs with 'References' as 'Implement' pairs with 'Guidelines'

understand.plain: The analogy asks: what is to References as Implement is to Guidelines? Guidelines are put into action by being 'implemented' — the verb associated with guidelines. Similarly, References are used by being 'cited' — the verb associated with references in academic or formal writing. 'Cite' means to quote or reference a source, making it the direct action associated with 'References'.

keywords.explain: To quote or refer to a source, text, or authority as evidence or justification; the primary action performed with references.
keywords.example: Please cite your sources at the end of the report.

keywords.explain: To put a plan, decision, or guideline into effect; the action performed with guidelines.
keywords.example: The company will implement new safety guidelines next month.

keywords.explain: A :: B means 'A is acted upon by / associated with B'. References -> cite; Guidelines -> implement.
keywords.example: Laws : Enforce :: Guidelines : Implement — the verb that acts on the noun.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a word analogy question.

given.aim: Find the word related to 'References' in the same way that 'Implement' is related to 'Guidelines'

terms.meaning: Guidelines are meant to be followed/put into practice, i.e., implemented
terms.example: Safety guidelines are implemented in the workplace
terms.connects: Establishes the relationship type: noun -> its associated action verb

terms.meaning: Citations or sources listed in academic/formal writing to credit prior work
terms.example: A bibliography or reference list at the end of a research paper
terms.connects: The noun whose associated action verb we need to find

terms.meaning: To mention or quote a reference as evidence; the canonical action performed with references
terms.example: In your essay, cite every source you used
terms.connects: The verb associated with References, parallel to Implement for Guidelines

given.plan: Identify the verb-relationship: Guidelines are implemented; References are cited. Match 'cite' to the References slot.

steps.title: Identify the analogy pattern
steps.apply: Guidelines : Implement = the noun is acted upon by/associated with the verb. Apply the same pattern: References : _____ = what verb is associated with references?
steps.note: The relationship type is 'noun : action performed with/on it'.

steps.title: Match the verb to References
steps.apply: You cite references (cite = to quote or attribute a source). Sight = to see/spot (unrelated). Site = a location (noun, unrelated). Plagiarise = to copy without attribution (opposite of citing properly).
steps.note: Cite is the only option that represents the proper action associated with References.

solution.result: C. Cite — just as guidelines are implemented, references are cited.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a word analogy question based on noun-verb relationships.

questions.question:
The question below consists of a pair of related words followed by four pairs of words. Select the pair that best expresses the relation in the original pair.

Unemployed : Worker

A. fallow : land  B. unaware : sleeper  C. wit : jester  D. renovated : house
questions.to_find: the word pair that best expresses the same relationship as Unemployed : Worker

understand.plain: An 'unemployed' person is a worker who is currently not being used for their purpose (not working). The relationship is: something is temporarily not fulfilling its function. 'Fallow' describes land that is ploughed but left unseeded for a season — land not being used for its purpose temporarily. This perfectly mirrors the Unemployed : Worker relationship. An unemployed person is a worker not currently working; fallow land is land not currently being farmed.

keywords.explain: A worker who is capable of working but is currently not in work; temporarily idle from their function.
keywords.example: An unemployed engineer is still an engineer — just not currently working.

keywords.explain: Land that is ploughed or tilled but left unsown or uncultivated for a season to restore its fertility; land not currently in productive use.
keywords.example: The farmer left the eastern field fallow this year to restore nutrients.

keywords.explain: The relationship: X describes a Y that is temporarily not performing its natural function. Unemployed:Worker and Fallow:Land both fit this pattern.
keywords.example: Unemployed worker = worker not working; Fallow land = land not farming.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal analogy question.

given.aim: Find the pair where the first word describes the second word as temporarily inactive/unused from its primary function

terms.meaning: An unemployed worker is a worker who is not currently doing work — temporarily idle from their function
terms.example: A doctor who is between jobs is an unemployed doctor
terms.connects: Base relationship: adjective describes a noun in a state of temporary non-use

terms.meaning: Fallow land is land that is not currently being cultivated — temporarily unused from its primary agricultural function
terms.example: Leaving a field fallow for one season to restore soil fertility
terms.connects: Parallel relationship: adjective describes a noun in temporary non-use

terms.meaning: A sleeper who is unaware — but 'unaware' is not a state of not fulfilling function; it is a state of consciousness
terms.example: An unaware sleeper is simply a sleeping person without consciousness — different relationship type
terms.connects: Eliminated: the relationship type differs from the base pair

given.plan: Identify the core relationship as 'adjective meaning temporarily not fulfilling its role : the noun that has that role'. Test each option against this template.

steps.title: Establish the base relationship
steps.apply: Unemployed : Worker = an X that is not doing what it is meant to do. The adjective 'unemployed' means the worker is temporarily without work. Relationship: [temporarily idle state] : [entity that has a function].
steps.note: The key is 'temporarily not fulfilling the natural function of the entity'.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.apply: A. fallow:land — fallow = land not under cultivation; MATCHES (land's function is to grow crops). B. unaware:sleeper — a sleeping person being unaware is normal/expected, not a state of not fulfilling function. C. wit:jester — wit IS the function of a jester, not absence of it. D. renovated:house — renovated means improved, not idle.
steps.note: Only fallow:land mirrors the 'temporarily not fulfilling function' relationship.

solution.result: A. fallow : land — just as an unemployed worker is a worker not currently working, fallow land is land not currently being cultivated.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a verbal analogy question testing semantic relationships between word pairs.
